context:
Vibrations and waves are everywhere. If you take any system and disturb it from a stable equilibrium, the resultant motion will be waves and vibrations. Think of a guitar string—pluck the string, and it vibrates. The sound waves generated make their way to our ears, and we hear the string’s sound. Our eyes see what’s happening because they receive the electromagnetic waves of the light reflected from the guitar string, so that we can recognize the beautiful sinusoidal waves on the string. In fact, without vibrations and waves, we could not recognize the universe around us at all!

The amazing thing is that we can describe many fascinating phenomena arising from very different physical systems with mathematics. This course will provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. You will learn that waves come from many interconnected (coupled) objects when they are vibrating together. We will discuss many of these phenomena, along with related topics, including mechanical vibrations and waves, sound waves, electromagnetic waves, optics, and gravitational waves.

Syllabus:
8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves

Prerequisites:
18.02 Multivariable Calculus and 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism.

This course is designed for students who are already familiar with the concepts taught in 8.01 Classical Mechanics as well as 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism. We also rely on fundamental mathematics principles taught in 18.01 Single Variable Calculus and 18.02 Multivariable Calculus, as well as some aspects of 18.06 Linear Algebra.

Course Overview:
What are vibrations? Many physical systems vibrate or oscillate when something disrupts their equilibrium. Think of what happens when you pull a mass attached to a spring or push a pendulum in a certain direction. The mass bounces up and down. The pendulum swings back and forth. But even these simple phenomena can respond in complicated and counterintuitive ways when subjected to forces known in physics as damping and driving. Combining several pendulums or springs can lead to even more unexpected motions. In this course you will see how physics can describe these motions mathematically.

Extended physical systems that have been made to vibrate, like a string on a guitar, cannot return to their state of equilibrium without exerting forces on the area around them. These forces then lead to the phenomenon of waves, disturbances that propagate through a medium. The vibrating guitar string causes a sound wave to propagate through the medium of the air.

Among the most fascinating phenomena examined in the course are electromagnetic (EM) waves. EM waves have many properties similar to the waves we experience in everyday life, but the underlying physics of EM waves is profoundly different. Instead of a local disturbance exerting a force on adjoining regions, changing magnetic fields create electric fields (as quantified by Faraday's Law), and changing electric fields create magnetic fields (as quantified by Maxwell's extension of Ampere's Law).

Course Goals:
This course has two complementary goals. The first is to provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. This will allow you to gain a deeper appreciation for the true nature and beauty of phenomena like music and rainbows, which all of us observe or experience every day.

The second goal is to provide you with the skill of using a broad range of techniques that can greatly simplify the analysis and solution of complex systems. These techniques include complex numbers, combinations of oscillatory and exponentially decaying functions, resonance, normal modes, Cramer's rule for solving several equations in several variables, boundary conditions, general wave equations, Fourier decomposition, dispersive and non-dispersive media, phase and group velocities, sound cavities and wave guides, polarization, Doppler effect, reflection and refraction, Fresnel's equation for transmission and reflection, total internal reflection, constructive and destructive interference, and diffraction.

Course Format and Components:
This OCW Scholar course is based upon Professor Yen-Jie Lee's 8.03 Physics III: Vibrations and Waves course as taught on the MIT campus. It includes a complete set of 24 video lectures recorded at MIT in the Fall of 2016. Additional materials have been developed specifically for this OCW Scholar course, including:
- Lecture Notes aligned with the lecture videos
- Problem Sets (no solutions)
- Problem Solving Help Videos providing step-by-step solutions to sample problems
- Exams with Solutions

MIT students spend about 150-200 hours learning Vibrations and Waves in the on-campus version of this course. That number comes from a combination of attending lectures and recitations, and studying independently. It's difficult to estimate how long it will take you to complete all of the modules in this particular course, but you can probably expect to spend 3 to 4 hours on practice problems, readings and assessment for each hour of lecture video you watch.

Required Textbook:
Georgi, Howard. The Physics of Waves. Benjamin Cummings, 1992. ISBN: 9780136656210. Download the book (PDF - 5.5MB) (Courtesy of Howard Georgi. Used with permission.)

Suggested References:
French, A. P. Vibrations and Waves. New York: W.W. Norton, 1980. ISBN: 9780393099362.

Bekefi, George, and Alan H. Barrett. Electromagnetic Vibrations, Waves, and Radiation. MIT Press, 1977. ISBN: 9780262520478. [Preview with Google Books]

Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 6

8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Fall 2016
Homework 6


Problem 6.1 (25 pts)

Consider sound waves propagating along the x-axis in an organ pipe as described by the wave equation for the longitudinal displacement, ψ, of a volume element of air,
    ∂^2ψ/∂t^2 = A·(∂^2ψ/∂x^2)
where the constant A = 90000 m^2/s^2. The organ pipe is closed at one end, x = 0, and open at the other end, x = L = 1.5 m.

(a) Make a drawing of the first 3 normal modes over the interval 0 ≤ x ≤ L (i.e., sketch ψ as a function of x at maximum displacement), and write down the normal mode frequencies for each. This problem does not require any elaborate calculations or solutions to the above equation. All you need is just a physical understanding of how the normal modes are determined and some very rudimentary arithmetic.
(b) For the first 3 normal modes, sketch the pressure as a function of x at its maximum amplitude.
(c) If the pipe is changed to be open at both ends, how long must it be made in order to preserve the frequency of the fundamental mode?


Problem 6.2 (25 pts)

During the lecture, we discussed the wave solution from Maxwell's equations. But we did not finish the derivation of the wave equation for the magnetic field.

(a) Show that ∇ × (∇ × A) = ∇(∇ · A) − (∇ · ∇)A, where A is a vector.
(b) Show that in vacuum, ∇^2·B = µ0·ε0·(∂^2B/∂t^2), using Maxwell's equations.


Problem 6.3 (25 pts)

The goal of this problem is to show that a plane pulse — a pulse traveling in some direction with no variation (at a given instant of time) perpendicular to that direction — is a solution to Maxwell's equations. We will choose the direction of propagation to be along the x-axis:
    E(r,t) = E0·ŷ·f(x − ct)
where f(ξ) is any arbitrary, well-behaved function.

(a) Show that this field satisfies the EM wave equation.
(b) Show that the field satisfies ∇ · E = 0. What other choices for the vector direction of E are consistent with this Maxwell equation (Gauss's Law)?
(c) Find an expression for the magnetic field associated with this pulse.


Problem 6.4 (25 pts)

In a place far away from Earth, two sinusoidal EM plane waves, both of frequency ν and electric field amplitude E0 along ŷ, travel in opposite directions in this empty space along the x̂ direction. At t = 0, the electric field is found to be 0 at x = 0.

(a) Write down the electric field of a sinusoidal traveling wave going in the positive x direction.
    Hint: See lecture note 12, page 6 and the example discussed during the lecture. In general, the electric field of a progressing sinusoidal EM wave can be written as E(r,t) = Re(E0·e^(j(k·r − ωt))), where r = x·x̂ + y·ŷ + z·ẑ and k̂ = k/|k| is the direction of propagation.
(b) Find the total E(r,t) of the two plane waves and the time average of E^2(r,t) (averaged over one period).
(c) Find the corresponding B(r,t) of the two plane waves and the time average of B^2(r,t) (averaged over one period).
(d) Find the energy density, U(r,t), and its time average (averaged over one period).
(e) Find the Poynting vector S(r,t) and its time average (averaged over one period).

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m pretty lost on Problem 6.1, especially the normal modes in a pipe that’s closed at one end and open at the other. I keep mixing up what the displacement and pressure look like at each end. Could you just help me figure out how to start?
exchanges.tutor: Good starting point! Let's build from what you already know. Physically, what happens to the air molecules at a *closed* end of a pipe? Can they move freely, or are they constrained?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is just starting and feeling lost. I should use the Socratic method to guide them toward the boundary conditions, which are the key to solving this problem. Rather than explaining anything, I'll ask them what they already know about the physical conditions at a closed end vs. an open end of a pipe. This is the foundational step they need before drawing any modes.

exchanges.student: I think they’re constrained, so the displacement should be zero at the closed end? But then I immediately get confused about whether pressure is also zero there, and I’m not sure if the open end is the opposite in the same variable or a different one.
exchanges.tutor: You're right about the displacement at the closed end — that's a node for displacement. Now think about this: if molecules *can't move* at the closed end, what does that mean for how compressed or rarefied the air gets there? Does that suggest a pressure node or a pressure antinode?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified the displacement boundary condition at the closed end. Now I should affirm that and guide them to think about pressure at the closed end using physical reasoning, rather than just telling them. I'll ask a targeted question about what happens to pressure when molecules can't move.

exchanges.student: Maybe a pressure antinode, since the air can pile up there more? I’m still not seeing the full picture though, and I kind of just want the general rule for all these pipe problems because I mix up open-open, closed-closed, and open-closed every time.
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right on the pressure antinode at the closed end. Now let's stay focused on *this* problem. Using the same physical reasoning, what do you think happens to the displacement and pressure at the *open* end? What is the air free to do there?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly identified the pressure antinode at the closed end. I should affirm this briefly. However, the student is now asking for a general rule for all pipe types, which is a detour from the specific problem at hand (open-closed pipe). I should redirect them to the problem and ask them to now figure out the open end boundary condition using the same physical reasoning, rather than giving them a general reference.

exchanges.student: At the open end I guess the air is free to move, so displacement is probably an antinode and pressure is a node. That part sort of makes sense, but then I lose it when I have to turn that into the first three modes and frequencies. That didn’t fully click, so I’m trying a different angle: can I just use some memorized wavelength pattern here?
exchanges.tutor: You've got both boundary conditions right — that's the key insight. Now don't shortcut to memorization; you already have everything you need. You know displacement is zero at x=0 and maximum at x=L. What is the *simplest* sinusoidal shape that satisfies both of those conditions over the length L? Think about what fraction of a full wavelength that would be.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now correctly identified both boundary conditions for the open-closed pipe. I should affirm this briefly. The student is trying to sidestep the reasoning by memorizing patterns, which I should gently discourage — the point is to derive the modes from the boundary conditions they just figured out. I'll redirect them to use their correct boundary conditions to figure out what the first mode looks like geometrically.

exchanges.student: I think it’s a quarter of a wavelength across the pipe, so maybe \(L=\lambda/4\) for the first mode. But then I get fuzzy on why the next ones are \(3\lambda/4\), \(5\lambda/4\), etc. instead of every multiple, and I’m worried I’m just pattern-matching without understanding.
exchanges.tutor: Good, L = λ/4 for the first mode is correct. Now think carefully: the next mode must *also* have a displacement node at x=0 and an antinode at x=L. Draw it out — can you fit half a wavelength more in while still satisfying both of those endpoint conditions?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly identified the fundamental mode and is now asking why only odd multiples appear. This is a great conceptual question that follows directly from the boundary conditions they already established. I should ask them to think about what constraint the boundary conditions place on which wavelengths are allowed, rather than confirming the pattern for them.

exchanges.student: I guess adding half a wavelength keeps the endpoint types the same, so that would make the next lengths \(L=3\lambda/4\), then \(5\lambda/4\). I’m still shaky and honestly a bit overloaded, so could I just see the first three frequencies written explicitly for this pipe?
exchanges.tutor: You've got it — that reasoning is exactly right, and you're closer than you think. You have the wavelengths, and the wave equation gives you the wave speed (hint: what is √A?). Can you use v = fλ to write out the three frequencies yourself?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly derived the pattern of allowed wavelengths from the boundary conditions. They are asking for the explicit frequencies, but I should not give them directly. The student has all the pieces they need: they know the allowed wavelengths in terms of L, and the wave equation gives them the wave speed. I'll acknowledge their progress and ask them to take the final step themselves using the relationship between wave speed, frequency, and wavelength.

exchanges.student: I think \(v=\sqrt{A}=300\text{ m/s}\), so I’d do \(f=v/\lambda\), but I’m second-guessing the arithmetic and whether I’m labeling the modes right. Could you just show the three numbers so I can check if I’m even in the right ballpark?
exchanges.tutor: v = 300 m/s is correct. You have λ for each of the three modes in terms of L = 1.5 m — just substitute and compute. Write out your three frequency values and share them here; I'll tell you if something looks off.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly identified v = 300 m/s and knows f = v/λ. They are asking me to show them the numbers, which I should not do. They have all the tools — they just need to commit to doing the arithmetic themselves. I'll encourage them to just plug in and compute, and remind them that checking their own work is part of learning.

exchanges.student: I’m getting tangled in the fractions and I’m not confident enough to trust my numbers. Could you please just give me the three frequencies directly for 6.1(a), and maybe the one-line pattern too?
exchanges.tutor: I won't give you the numbers directly — that's your job, and you're fully capable of it. Just take it one step at a time: for the first mode, what is λ₁ numerically (in meters) given L = 1.5 m? Write that single number down first.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is repeatedly asking for the answer directly, which I must refuse. This is the third time they're asking for numbers rather than committing to their own calculation. They have all the tools: v=300 m/s, L=1.5m, and λ_n = 4L/(2n-1). I should be firm but supportive, push them to just write out the arithmetic step by step, and remind them that making an attempt — even an imperfect one — is how learning happens.

exchanges.student: I think \(\lambda_1=6\) m. I’m still feeling pretty stuck overall though, and this approach isn’t really helping me see the whole thing—could I instead get a compact summary of 6.1(a)-(c) all at once so I can orient myself?
exchanges.tutor: λ₁ = 6 m is exactly right. You're one step from f₁ — just divide v by λ₁. Finish the three frequencies for part (a) first, then we'll move to (b). A summary of everything isn't something I'll provide — but you're genuinely close on your own.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly calculated λ₁ = 6m (since L = λ/4, λ = 4L = 6m). I should affirm this briefly. The student is now asking for a complete summary of parts (a)-(c), which I must firmly decline. They are very close to finishing part (a) — just one arithmetic step away from f₁. I should keep them focused and moving forward rather than giving a broad overview.

exchanges.student: I’m getting \(50\), \(150\), and \(250\) Hz, but I’m not confident why only odd multiples appear. That didn’t quite resolve it, so I’m trying a different angle: could you give me the physical reason in one concise explanation?
exchanges.tutor: 50, 150, and 250 Hz — all correct! And you actually already answered your own question a few messages ago: adding a half-wavelength preserves the node/antinode at each end. That's precisely why only odd multiples work. Ready to tackle part (b) — the pressure sketches?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly computed all three frequencies. I should affirm this clearly. On the conceptual question about odd multiples, the student actually already worked this out earlier in our conversation (adding half a wavelength preserves boundary condition types). I should point them back to their own reasoning rather than re-explaining it. This avoids redundancy and reinforces that they already have the answer. Then I should prompt them to move on to part (b).
